full_notes:
Tribals Dikus and the Vision of a Golden Age is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that tribals, Dikus and the Vision of a Golden Age 4 In 1895, a man named Birsa was seen roaming the forests and villages of Chottanagpur in Jharkhand.

The text explains that people said he had miraculous powers – he could cure all diseases and multiply grain.

The text explains that birsa himself declared that God had appointed him to save his people from trouble, free them from the slavery of dikus (outsiders).

The text explains that soon thousands began following Birsa, believing that he was bhagwan (God) and had come to solve all their problems.

The text explains that birsa was born in a family of Mundas – a tribal group that lived in Chottanagpur.

The text explains that but his followers included other tribals of the region – Santhals and Oraons.

The text explains that all of them in different ways were unhappy with the changes they were experiencing and the problems they were facing under British rule.

The text explains that their familiar ways of life seemed to be disappearing, their livelihoods were under threat, and their religion appeared to be in danger.

The text explains that who were the outsiders being referred to as dikus, and how did they enslave the people of the region?

The text explains that what was happening to the tribal people under the British?

The text explains that these are some of the questions you will read about in this chapter.

The text explains that you have read about tribal societies last year.

The text explains that most tribes had customs and rituals that were very different from those laid down by Brahmans.

The text explains that these societies also did not have the sharp social divisions that were characteristic of caste societies.

The text explains that all those who belonged to the same tribe thought of themselves as sharing common ties of kinship.

The text explains that however, this did not mean that there were no social and economic differences within tribes.

The text explains that you will read more about them in your history book next year.

The text explains that adapted from Verrier Elwin, Baiga (1939) and Elwin’s unpublished ‘Notes on the Khonds’ (Verrier Elwin Papers, Nehru Memorial Museum and Library) Source 1 Fig.

The text explains that they began to use the plough, and gradually got rights over the land they lived on.

The text explains that therefore, all of them had rights on the land.

The text explains that they enjoyed a certain amount of economic power and had the right to administer and control their territories.

The text explains that the British also wanted a regular revenue source for the state.

The text explains that so they introduced land settlements – that is, they measured the land, defined the rights of each individual to that land, and fixed the revenue demand for the state.

The text explains that as you have seen (Chapter 2), the tenants were to pay rent to the landowner who in turn paid revenue to the state.

The text explains that facing widespread protests, the British had to ultimately allow them the right to carry on shifting cultivation in some parts of the forest.

The text explains that tRIBALS, DIKUS AND THE VISION OF A GOLDEN AGE 45 Forest laws and their impact The life of tribal groups, as you have seen, was directly connected to the forest.

The text explains that in this land of the English how hard it is to live How hard it is to live In the village sits the landlord In the gate sits the Kotwar In the garden sits the Patwari In the field sits the government In this land of the English how hard it is to live To pay cattle tax we have to sell cow To pay forest tax we have to sell buffalo To pay land tax we have to sell bullock How are we to get our food?

The text explains that source 2 Sleeper – The horizontal planks of wood on which railway lines are laid

The text explains that check how many people die in mines every year, and what are the reasons for their death. 

The text explains that they talked of a golden age when the Mundas had been free of the oppression of dikus, and said there would be a time when the ancestral right of the community would be restored.

The text explains that there too he heard it said that it was possible for the Mundas to attain the Kingdom of Heaven, and regain their lost rights.

The text explains that his movement was aimed at reforming tribal society.

The text explains that what worried British officials most was the political aim of the Birsa movement, for it wanted to drive out missionaries, moneylenders, landlords, and the government and set up a Munda Raj with Birsa at its head.

The text explains that tribals, dikus and the vision of a golden age 49 The land policies of the British were destroying their traditional land system, landlords and moneylenders were taking over their land, and missionaries were criticising their traditional culture.

The text explains that first – it forced the colonial government to introduce laws so that the land of the tribals could not be easily taken over by dikus.

The text explains that in a meeting with British officials you try to explain the kinds of problems you face.

The text explains that what problems did shifting cultivators face under British rule?

The text explains that how did the powers of tribal chiefs change under colonial rule?

The text explains that what accounts for the anger of the tribals against the dikus?

The text explains that why do you think such a vision appealed to the people of the region?

The text explains that find out from your parents, friends or teachers, the names of some heroes of other tribal revolts in the twentieth century.

The text explains that choose any tribal group living in India today.

The text explains that find out about their customs and way of life, and how their lives have changed in the last 50 years.

The text explains that let’s imagine Imagine you are a jhum cultivator living in a forest village in the nineteenth century.

The text explains that you have just been told that the land you were born on no longer belongs to you.

Source: NCERT Class 8 Our Pasts III, Chapter 4: Tribals Dikus and the Vision of a Golden Age

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Tribals Dikus and the Vision of a Golden Age, which statement is supported by the source text about 44 OUR PASTS – III?
options: By the nineteenth century, tribal people in different parts of India were involved in a variety of activities.
Some were jhum cultivators Some of them practised jhum cultivation, that is, shifting cultivation.
This was done on small patches of land, mostly in forests.
The cultivators cut the treetops to allow sunlight to reach the ground, and burnt the vegetation on the land to clear it for cultivation.
